column2: Navigating UAD 3.6: Appraisers Compare Notes
column3: This session gives residential appraisers a practical forum to compare notes on preparing for UAD 3.6 and the changes it may bring to daily appraisal work. Rather than focusing on theory alone, the discussion is designed to help appraisers think through how the new standard may affect reporting, data collection, communication, and workflow in the real world.
column22: Chief Appraiser at ATW Appraisal Service
column23: State Certified Residential Real Estate Appraiser and owner of ATW Appraisal Service, where he has provided residential valuation services since 2011. Certified since 2007, he is on the VA roster and specializes in USDA rural housing, FHA, Jumbo, New Construction, and non-lender appraisal work. His background also includes eminent domain appraisal work with the North Carolina Department of Transportation and a wide range of residential assignments across North Carolina.

column2: Adding Pre-Listing & Pre-Purchase Appraisals: A Smarter Way to Grow Revenue
column3:
This session explains how pre-listing and pre-purchase appraisals can be added to an appraisal practice as a practical, defensible, and repeatable revenue stream.

Many appraisers understand that agents and buyers want better pricing insight, but struggle with how to offer these services without creating risk, confusion, or ethical concerns. This session breaks down how pre-listing and pre-purchase appraisals actually work in practice — from scope and reporting to positioning and agent communication.

The goal is to help appraisers add these services confidently, professionally, and in a way that strengthens — rather than dilutes — their brand.
column23:
Allison Rodriguez is a Certified Residential Appraiser with experience valuing a wide range of residential properties, including single-family homes, complex residential assignments, and multifamily properties. She specializes in complex valuation matters, serving a diverse clientele that includes realtors, brokers, consumers, attorneys, and other intended users requiring credible, well-supported opinions of value.

In addition to her appraisal practice, Allison teaches appraisal-related education courses for real estate professionals, helping realtors better understand valuation principles, market analysis, and the appraisal process. She is passionate about bridging the gap between the appraisal and real estate communities through education and practical insight.

A significant portion of her complex appraisal work involves properties positioned for potential sale. For assignments that extend beyond the residential scope, Allison collaborates with experienced commercial appraisal teams to ensure clients receive comprehensive and competent service.

Rodriguez is an active member of the Association of Texas Appraisers and the National Association of Appraisers, and she currently serves on the Membership Committee for both organizations. She remains committed to professional involvement and industry advancement.

Currently working toward her Certified General designation, Allison is dedicated to continuing education and maintaining the highest professional standards. She takes pride in delivering clear, reliable valuation services that help clients make informed and confident decisions.

column2: Beyond Comps: Advanced Pricing Strategies for Competitive Markets
column3:
This session goes beyond traditional comparable selection to explore how experienced professionals price properties accurately in competitive, fast-moving, and emotionally charged markets.

In many markets, relying solely on closed sales can lead to missed pricing signals, unnecessary price reductions, or credibility gaps with clients. This session introduces advanced pricing strategies that integrate market dynamics, buyer behavior, and competitive positioning — while staying grounded in evidence and defensibility.

The goal is to help attendees develop stronger pricing judgment and communicate pricing decisions with confidence in challenging market environments.

column2: Marketing 101: Clear Branding for Appraisers
column3:
This session introduces appraisers to the fundamentals of effective marketing — not from an advertising or hype-driven perspective, but from a clarity and positioning standpoint.

Many appraisers struggle with marketing not because they lack skill, but because their message is unclear: who they serve, what problems they solve, and why they are trusted. Drawing on the StoryBrand framework, this session explains how simple, clear messaging helps professionals stand out without sounding salesy or unprofessional.

The goal is to give appraisers a practical foundation for improving their brand presence using clear language, consistent positioning, and small, high-impact actions.
column22: Messaging & Marketing Expert for Service Businesses
column23:
Brad has been a Certified StoryBrand Guide since 2018, and has worked in world of media and communications since 2005. Over the last 15 years, he has helped numerous leaders simplify their complicated ideas to create effective marketing and compelling communication campaigns that drive results.

Brad has a Masters in Media & Communication from Dallas Seminary. He cut his teeth in the most communication-heavy industry on the planet (the American church), and has since worked with CEOs, VPs, and Founders in multiple industries to find the right words and put them to work to grow their business.

column2: Going from 100% Lender Work to 70% Private Work
column3:
This session provides a practical, experience-based roadmap for residential appraisers who want to reduce reliance on lender work and build a sustainable private-client practice.

Rather than presenting theory or marketing tactics in isolation, the session walks through a real business transition — from nearly 100% lender-based assignments to more than 70% private work — explaining what changed, what didn’t, and what actually worked over time.

The goal is to help appraisers understand how to reposition their services, workflows, and client relationships to serve private clients without abandoning professional rigor or burning bridges with lender work.
column23: I help growth-minded appraisers grow a thriving business by working less and earning more - while earning 5-star reviews. Want to know exactly how I have transformed and grown my appraisal business while many struggle? It’s not what most appraisers think, and it’s definitely not what they’re teaching in continuing education classes. Let's chat and I will share proven strategies you can begin implementing right away :)
column30: Member of ASA & NAR
column37: Appraisers leave with a clear, realistic framework for moving from predominantly lender-based work to a majority private-client practice — including how to define the right services, position them credibly, and manage the transition without sacrificing quality, professionalism, or trust.

column2: So You Want to Be an Appraiser? What the Job Really Is — and Why It Matters
column3:
This session introduces the appraisal profession to people who are curious about valuation as a career but don’t yet understand what appraisers actually do — or why the role matters.

Rather than focusing on licensing rules or technical detail, the session explains the real job of an appraiser: how appraisers think, what problems they solve, and why independent valuation plays a critical role in real estate markets, lending, and personal financial decisions.

The goal is to help attendees decide whether appraisal could be a meaningful and realistic career path for them.
column23: Cathy Putegnat is the Owner and Appraiser at Homestead Appraisals, Inc., serving clients across Oregon and Washington for more than two decades. Since founding her firm in 2001, she has built a steady, client-focused appraisal practice grounded in experience, consistency, and regional market knowledge. With over 20 years leading her own company, Cathy brings practical insight and long-term perspective to residential valuation, serving property owners, lenders, and real estate professionals throughout the Pacific Northwest.
column37: Aspiring Appraisers leave with a clear, honest understanding of what appraisers do, why their work matters, and whether valuation could be a good fit for their skills, interests, and career goals.
column40: So You Want to Be an Appraiser? What the Job Really Is—and Why It Matters

column2: Running a Modern Appraisal Firm Under UAD 3.6
column3:
This session explores what a modern appraisal firm actually looks like as the industry moves toward 2027 — and how firms are adapting their operations, staffing, and workflows under UAD 3.6.

As reporting becomes more structured and expectations around data quality, turnaround, and consistency increase, appraisal firms are being forced to rethink how they operate at scale. This session provides a firm-operator perspective on what is changing internally, where friction is showing up in the UAD 3.6 transition, and how leading firms are adjusting to remain efficient, compliant, and competitive.

The focus is operational reality, not theory.
column23:
Chad’s influence in the residential valuation space as a certified appraiser has evolved through the years from performing appraisals and mentoring trainee appraisers to co-founding and operating direct-appraisal companies, appraisal management companies, and franchise systems for the past 22 years. He takes a fundamental approach to business delivering value add products and services tailored for customers and delivered by a passionate, engaged workforce.

For more than two decades, Chad has dedicated his career to the real estate appraisal industry. His deep rooted respect for the appraisal profession permeates the Velox Valuations business and guides its commitment to delivering best-in-class services.

A native to the Hoosier State and a graduate of Indiana University, Chad enjoys adventure vacationing with his wife of 28 years and three children.
column30: NAA, MBA, Certified Appraiser
column37: Appraisers leave with a clearer understanding of how appraisal firms are evolving under UAD 3.6 — and practical insight into the operational realities shaping modern, resilient appraisal businesses as expectations continue to rise.

column2: After the “Low” Appraisal: Practical Strategies for Agents and Clients
column3:
This session equips agents and their clients with clear, realistic strategies for navigating transactions after an appraisal comes in below contract price.

“Low” appraisals are often treated as failures or conflicts, when in reality they are risk signals within a lending system designed to protect borrowers and capital. Drawing on experience across appraisal practice, lender operations, GSE policy, and valuation leadership, Chris Hunt explains what options actually exist after a low appraisal—and which ones waste time or increase risk.

The goal is to replace frustration and guesswork with informed decision-making and calm, professional communication.
column23:
Chris Hunt brings more than 25 years of leadership experience across the valuation landscape to his role as Managing Director of ValuationNEXUS. A Certified Residential Appraiser, Chris has held senior positions at Fannie Mae and appraisal management companies including Clear Capital, and most recently served as Chief Operating Officer of International Valuation Group (IVG).

With a career encompassing appraisal expertise, operational excellence, and technology innovation, Chris leads the strategy and execution of ValuationNEXUS AMC operations. His focus is on enhancing speed, quality, consistency, and transparency while reinforcing the independence and reliability of the valuation process.

Known for his people-first mindset and ability to balance strategic vision with hands-on execution, Chris is committed to building processes and systems that support the vision of ValuationNEXUS to deliver faster, clearer, and more trusted valuation outcomes.
column30: Various Certified Appraiser Licenses
column37: Real Estate Agents leave with a clear framework for handling low appraisals, understanding what levers exist, how decisions are evaluated, and how to guide clients through outcomes calmly and effectively.

column2: Valuation on Trial: Emerging Case Law and Legal Defense Strategies for Appraisers and AMCs
column3:
This Main Stage session examines how litigation, enforcement actions, and emerging case law are reshaping the risk landscape for appraisers and appraisal management companies.

As valuation reports are increasingly scrutinized in legal, regulatory, and enforcement contexts, understanding how appraisal work is evaluated after the fact has become critical. This session provides a practical, non-alarmist view into where appraisers and AMCs are being challenged, what patterns are emerging in recent cases, and how defensible valuation practice is evolving.

The focus is educational and forward-looking, helping valuation professionals better understand legal risk without providing legal advice.
Educational content only — confirm requirements with counsel and applicable regulators.
column12: Real Estate Appraisers, Aspiring Appraisers, Review Appraisers, AMC Personnel, Collateral Risk Professionals
column23: Craig Capilla is a trial lawyer with Franklin, Greenswag, Channon & Capilla, LLC, concentrating his practice in a wide range of civil litigation, including professional liability and commercial claims. He has successfully defended residential and commercial appraisers, appraisal firms, and appraisal management companies accused of negligence or improper activity in their valuation services. Craig has also represented numerous licensed professionals in state licensing matters including real estate brokers, real estate appraisers, appraisal management companies, mortgage loan originators and brokers, and others. He has represented clients in both state and federal lawsuits as well as licensing matters in a number of states including Illinois, Wisconsin, Minnesota, Maryland, Idaho, Michigan, Colorado, Indiana, Kentucky, Kansas, Tennessee, Delaware, and California. Craig is authorized to practice law in Illinois, Wisconsin, several Federal districts, the United States Court of Appeals, 7th Circuit, and is a member of the trial bar of the United States District Court for the Northern District of Illinois.
column37: Attendees leave with a grounded understanding of where legal and enforcement scrutiny most often focuses in valuation work — and a clearer sense of how sound judgment, documentation, and scope discipline support defensible appraisal practice over time.

column2: What the Best Appraisers Have in Common: Ethics, Judgment, and Professional Trust
column3:
This session examines what truly separates the best appraisers from the rest — not technical skill alone, but ethics, judgment, and professional trust.

While rules, standards, and tools are essential, long-term credibility in valuation is built on how appraisers think, decide, and act when faced with pressure, ambiguity, or incentives to compromise. This session explores ethics not as a compliance checkbox, but as a daily professional discipline.

The goal is to help appraisers at every stage reflect on what it means to be trusted — and how that trust shapes the future of the profession.
column23: Craig Capilla is a trial lawyer with Franklin, Greenswag, Channon & Capilla, LLC, concentrating his practice in a wide range of civil litigation, including professional liability and commercial claims. He has successfully defended residential and commercial appraisers, appraisal firms, and appraisal management companies accused of negligence or improper activity in their valuation services. Craig has also represented numerous licensed professionals in state licensing matters including real estate brokers, real estate appraisers, appraisal management companies, mortgage loan originators and brokers, and others. He has represented clients in both state and federal lawsuits as well as licensing matters in a number of states including Illinois, Wisconsin, Minnesota, Maryland, Idaho, Michigan, Colorado, Indiana, Kentucky, Kansas, Tennessee, Delaware, and California. Craig is authorized to practice law in Illinois, Wisconsin, several Federal districts, the United States Court of Appeals, 7th Circuit, and is a member of the trial bar of the United States District Court for the Northern District of Illinois.
column37: Aspiring Appraisers leave with a clearer understanding of why ethics and judgment - not just technical skill - define professional excellence, and how cultivating trust from the very start of a career is the most durable advantage an appraiser can have.

column2: The Power of Referrals
column3: This session explores how appraisers can turn referrals into a consistent source of private and non-lender business. It focuses on how trusted relationships, visibility, and follow-through can create a stronger pipeline of work beyond traditional lender channels.
column22: Owner, Appraisal Referral Network, Chief Appraiser and Owner, Empire Appraisal Group
column23: He is a Certified Residential Appraiser in Florida and owner of Empire Appraisal Group and Appraisal Referral Network. He has built his appraisal business primarily outside of traditional lending work by working with attorneys, real estate agents, and private clients on assignments including divorce, estate, partition, expert witness matters, and listing-related services. With over 20 years of experience and more than 10,000 residential appraisals completed, he provides practical insight based on real-world valuation experience. Through the Appraisal Referral Network, he helps appraisers grow private and non-lender work through education, referrals, and marketing and networking guidance.
column30: Daniel holds the MNAA and the RAA appraisal designations
column37: Attendees leave with a clearer understanding of how to build referral-driven business, strengthen the professional relationships that lead to private work, and create a more reliable pipeline of non-lender opportunities.

column2: Marketing for Appraisal Offices: What Actually Drives Business Growth
column3:
This isn’t the usual “upgrade your website,” “set up a Google Business Profile,” or “use AI for social media” marketing talk. This session provides a practitioner-led, no-fluff look at what appraisal offices need to be doing to market themselves effectively.

The presentation breaks down five core marketing areas every appraisal business owner should be actively managing, with practical tactics that can be adopted gradually and tailored to each office’s capacity and goals.

The idea is to help appraisal offices move from passive visibility to intentional, professional presence—building a stronger referral pipeline and more consistent demand over time.
column23:
David is a Pennsylvania & New Jersey State Certified Residential Appraiser serving a wide range of clients, including government agencies, financial institutions, law offices, estate planners, investors, and property owners. 

David founded Valiant Appraisal Services with a mission: to deliver appraisals built for real-world impact. Blending rigorous analysis, deep industry experience, and clear, human-centered communication, he helps clients confidently navigate high-stakes real estate decisions.

Outside of work, David enjoys spending his time with his wife and daughter, as well as hiking and playing pickleball.
column30: Lower Bucks Chamber of Commerce
column37: Appraisers will leave with a clear, practical framework for marketing their office - how to increase visibility, strengthen referral relationships, and build consistent growth.

column2: When Data Is Everywhere: Why Appraisers Matter More in an AI World
column3:
This session reframes the role of the appraiser in an environment where data, models, and automated insights are increasingly abundant.

As AI expands access to information, the perceived value of raw data declines — while the value of interpretation, judgment, and contextual understanding increases. This session explores why appraisers are not being displaced by data-rich systems, but instead are becoming more critical as interpreters, validators, and risk-aware professionals.

The conversation helps attendees understand how professional relevance shifts in an AI-driven landscape — and why human judgment remains essential when data is everywhere.
column23: With 20 years  experience and a passion for residential real estate; the Curd Brothers strive to be leaders in residential real estate appraisal, investment, and consultancy.   Through adaptation of cutting edge technology, consistent education, and 20 years performing in their markets; Taylor and Gavin believe in constant improvement and the pursuit of expertise.
column37: Appraisers leave with a clearer understanding of why appraisers matter more — not less — in an AI-driven world, and how professional judgment, context, and accountability remain the true differentiators when data is abundant.

column2: Analytics-Backed Adjustments: Turning Market Data Into Defensible Decisions
column3:
This session focuses on how appraisers can use market analytics to support adjustments that are both credible and defensible.

As scrutiny around appraisal adjustments increases, relying on intuition or unsupported rules of thumb is becoming less acceptable. This session provides a practical framework for turning market data into adjustment support that holds up under review, litigation, and quality control.

Led by a long-time appraisal analyst and educator, the session bridges statistical insight with real-world appraisal application — helping appraisers strengthen analysis without overcomplicating their workflow.
column23:
George Dell, SRA, MAI, ASA, CRE
George consults and trains in the unification of data science, artificial intelligence, and expert judgment for valuation and asset collateral risk.  The EBV (Evidence Based Valuation)© approach brings reproducibility and authentication to valuation, and enables measurable risk/reliability scoring of valuations.  The georgedell.com weekly blog, raises often-controversial issues.  The related valuemetrics.info website provides information on upcoming classes.  He also publishes TAAR (The Asset Analyst Report), and the CIAR (Collateral Investor Assay Report).
column30: SRA, MAI, ASA, CRE, LAI, CDEI,
column37: Appraisers leave with a clearer understanding of how market data can support appraisal adjustments — and practical insight into turning analytics into clear, defensible valuation decisions.

column2: Life After Certification: What the First Years Really Look Like
column3:
This session provides an unfiltered look at what actually happens after certification — the part of the career path that is often glossed over.

Many aspiring appraisers focus intensely on getting licensed or certified, only to discover that the transition to independent practice brings new challenges: confidence gaps, workflow questions, business decisions, and real-world pressure. This session centers on lived experience from appraisers who have recently crossed that threshold.
The goal is to replace assumptions with reality and help newer appraisers feel better prepared for what comes next.
column22: Chief Appraiser & Valuation Leader | Founder of Texas Residential Valuation Society & American Valuation Society
column23:
I am the Chief Appraiser at Clear Falls Valuations, where I lead residential valuation quality, independence, and regulatory compliance. My career spans more than 20 years, including roles at Fannie Mae, Wells Fargo, and now Cornerstone Capital Bank. 

I specialize in USPAP, FIRREA, GSE guidelines, and appraiser panel management. 

As the founder of the Texas Residential Valuation Society and the American Valuation Society, I am committed to elevating education, collaboration, and modernization within the valuation and mortgage industries.
column37: Aspiring Appraisers leave with a realistic understanding of what the first years after certification actually look like — including common challenges, mindset shifts, and practical guidance that helps new appraisers navigate early responsibilities withgreater confidence and clarity.

column2: What's Getting Appraisers Sued: Risk and Liability in Today's Market
column3: This session is designed to give appraisers a practical view of where liability risk is coming from in today’s market and how they can better protect themselves in day-to-day practice. Drawing from current claims, complaint patterns, and risk management experience, the session will focus on the issues most likely to create exposure for independent appraisers and small firms, including how changing reporting standards such as UAD 3.6 may affect that risk.

Peck is a licensed insurance broker in 50 states and holds a Master’s Degree in Accounting.

column2: AIVRE Demo: AI-Assisted Valuation Workflows in Practice
column3:
This demo shows how AIVRE applies AI to support appraisal workflows—helping appraisers organize data, surface insights, and reduce friction—without automating value conclusions or replacing professional judgment.

The goal is to give attendees a realistic understanding of where AI actually helps today, how it integrates with appraisal practice, and what responsibilities remain squarely with the appraiser.
column23: Jake Lew is the Co-Founder and CEO of Aivre, a pioneering real estate technology company leveraging artificial intelligence to modernize and streamline the appraisal process. Under his leadership, Aivre became the first GSE-verified software vendor for UAD 3.6. Jake is also a Certified Residential Appraiser, bringing firsthand industry expertise and a deep understanding of appraisal workflows to Aivre’s product innovation.
column30: Certified Residential Appraiser
column37: Real Estate Appraisers leave with a grounded understanding of how AI can augment appraisal work today - speeding up analysis and organization - while preserving independence, accountability, and judgment.

column2: DataMaster Demo: Faster Data Entry, Fewer Errors, Better Workflow
column3:
This demo shows how DataMaster is used to dramatically reduce data entry time and errors in appraisal reporting by pulling verified MLS, public record, and market data directly into appraisal software.

The goal is to give attendees a clear, realistic understanding of how DataMaster fits into daily appraisal workflows—especially for appraisers who want to spend less time typing and more time analyzing.
column23:
Jared is a Certified Residential Appraiser and owner of J. Leland & Company, serving Utah’s Wasatch Front for more than 30 years . His firm provides objective valuation and consulting services across lending, estate settlement, divorce, bankruptcy, investment, and solar/green assignments.

Jared also serves as Chief Appraiser and Director of Product Development at DataMaster, helping shape appraisal technology used nationwide to improve efficiency and accuracy.

A Certified Residential Instructor with expertise in solar valuation, data analysis, and highest and best use , he blends deep field experience with innovation, education, and leadership within the profession.
column24: Chief Appraiser and Director Product Development
column37: Real Estate Appraisers leave with a clear understanding of how DataMaster helps appraisers work faster and cleaner—by removing repetitive data entry without compromising analysis or independence.

column2: Professional Life of an Appraiser: From Assignment to Delivery
column3:
This session provides a candid, start-to-finish look at the professional life of an appraiser — from accepting an assignment through delivery, revision cycles, and client communication — as experienced by professionals with more than 20 years in the field.

Rather than focusing on theory or entry-level guidance, this panel explores what appraisal work actually feels like over the long term: how roles evolve, what parts of the job become more meaningful, what parts remain challenging, and how experienced appraisers adapt to changes in markets, technology, and client expectations.

The goal is to give attendees a realistic picture of appraisal as a career, not just a credential.
column23:
Jason is a certified residential appraiser and owner of Titan Appraisal, LLC dba Velox Valuations Nashville; a first of its kind, franchise model in the appraisal space. He is a boots on the ground appraiser building his non-lender appraisal clientele and completing assignments for lenders, banks and AMC's across the country in his market of Nashville, TN.

Based on 27 years of industry experience and 21 years of appraisal experience, Jason is a self-starter and entrepreneur with strong leadership skills including teaching, training and mentoring with exceptional interpersonal skills including listening and communicating, encouraging and motivating others, negotiating, and fostering strong business relationships while staying eager and able to learn new concepts and skills quickly while also staying abreast of the latest industry trends and issues.

Jason's experience in the profession includes things like construction, building and framing. He has experience as a loan officer and has owned and operated an AMC. He has ran and operated a large regional bank appraisal management system and has served on the Tennessee Real Estate Appraisal Commission. Jason has been invited as speaker and presenter as an industry expert for Appraisers, Banks, Lenders, Mortgage companies and Real Estate Brokers and Agents. His most recent presentation was at the 2023 NAA Appraisal Summit on “Growing your Non-Lending work”. Jason returned to the field as a working appraiser in 2015

Jason is a designated member of the NAA and was appointed as a Board Member Pro Tem in 2022 by then, current President Bryan Reynolds and re-elected in 2023 and have served on the membership and fundraising committees is currently serving as the membership committee, chair. Jason has attended and participated in the 2021 ACTS conference in Bay St. Louis and the 2021 Appraisal Summit and the 2022 ACT's conference in Charleston, SC. and Appraisal Summit and 2023 ACT’s conference in Sacramento, CA. and Appraisal Summit. column2: NightHawk and the Next Generation of Appraisal Reporting
column3:
This demo showcases how NightHawk represents a structural shift in appraisal reporting — moving
beyond traditional forms-based workflows to a modern, analysis-first approach where the appraisal report is automatically generated from the appraiser’s inputs, reasoning, and conclusions.

The goal is to give attendees a grounded, practical understanding of what the future of appraisal
reporting looks like when technology supports the appraiser’s judgment rather than forcing it into static
forms.
column23:
Jeff Bradford is the founder and CEO at Bradford Technologies. His company has been serving appraisers for 38 years.

Jeff has been recognized by the CRN as a Valuation Visionary and a Tech All Star by the MBA. Most recently, he was recognized by the NAA with the President’s Award and voted Mentor of the Year in 2025 by the Appraisal Buzz community.

He has earned three Masters degrees. Has worked at companies such as Apple and Structural Dynamics Research Corporation and in his spare time, he enjoys adventure travel with his daughter Whitney. They have dog sledded at the North Pole, visited Antarctica and climbed Mt. Kilimanjaro among other adventures around the world.
column37: Attendees leave with a clear understanding of how NightHawk redefines appraisal reporting, demonstrating how a non-forms-based, data-driven approach can better align with professional judgment, improve efficiency, and prepare appraisal practices for the future.

column2: Market Moves with Mark and Jeff: AI in Modern Appraisal Practice
column3:
This live recording of Market Moves brings a trusted industry voice directly to the ValuSignal audience to
examine how AI is actually showing up in modern appraisal practice. The session focuses on practical use
cases, professional judgment, and where technology genuinely adds—or complicates—value in appraisal
workflows.
Rather than speculative futurism, this conversation centers on what appraisers are encountering now and
how leading practitioners are thinking about adoption, risk, and defensibility.
column23:
Jeff Morley is a Certified General Appraiser in Utah and Nevada and holds the MAI and SRA designations from the Appraisal Institute since July 2014. He has extensive commercial and residential appraisal experience. Jeff has been appraising full time since 2002. Jeff was appointed to a 4-year term on the Utah Appraiser Licensing and Appraisal Board in 2016. He has served as a tax appeal hearing officer for the Board of Equalization in Washington and Iron Counties since 2008.

He specializes in the appraisal of recreational properties in Brian Head, Duck Creek, Panguitch Lake and the surrounding areas. Jeff has experience in appraising properties in Washington, Iron, Kane, Garfield and Beaver counties in Utah and in Clark County, Nevada.

In addition to the appraisal of general commercial properties, Jeff has experience in the appraisal of lodging facilities, restaurants, RV parks, subdivisions, apartment housing and large tracts of land. He also has experience in eminent domain, appraisal work of fee and partial interest acquisitions for Right-of-Way, and utility line easements. Jeff is on the UDOT list of approved appraisers for complex properties.

column2: A Practical Guide to Deploying AI
column3:
This session offers a practical, operations-focused perspective on how AI is being deployed
inside a large, multi-service valuation and appraisal organization.

Rather than treating AI as a standalone technology initiative, the session explores how AI fits
into real day-to-day practice — supporting people, processes, and decision-making without
disrupting core professional responsibilities.

By grounding the conversation in organizational reality, this session helps attendees understand
how AI adoption actually happens at scale, what tradeoffs are involved, and how to think
pragmatically about implementation rather than experimentation alone.
column23:
Jeff Morley is a Certified General Appraiser in Utah and Nevada and holds the MAI and SRA designations from the Appraisal Institute since July 2014. He has extensive commercial and residential appraisal experience. Jeff has been appraising full time since 2002. Jeff was appointed to a 4-year term on the Utah Appraiser Licensing and Appraisal Board in 2016. He has served as a tax appeal hearing officer for the Board of Equalization in Washington and Iron Counties since 2008.

He specializes in the appraisal of recreational properties in Brian Head, Duck Creek, Panguitch Lake and the surrounding areas. Jeff has experience in appraising properties in Washington, Iron, Kane, Garfield and Beaver counties in Utah and in Clark County, Nevada.

In addition to the appraisal of general commercial properties, Jeff has experience in the appraisal of lodging facilities, restaurants, RV parks, subdivisions, apartment housing and large tracts of land. He also has experience in eminent domain, appraisal work of fee and partial interest acquisitions for Right-of-Way, and utility line easements. Jeff is on the UDOT list of approved appraisers for complex properties.

column2:
Ask the Appraiser: A Live Agent–Appraiser
Panel Experience
column3:
This session tackles one of the most challenging areas of residential valuation: pricing
properties that do not fit neatly into standard models, neighborhoods, or datasets.

“Oddball” properties—unique designs, atypical locations, mixed uses, unconventional
improvements, or market outliers—often expose the limits of formulaic comp selection. This
session explains how experienced appraisers approach these assignments with disciplined
judgment, creative analysis, and defensible logic.

The goal is to help attendees price non-standard homes with confidence, clarity, and
professional credibility.
column23:
John Copulos is the owner of Copulos and Associates Real Estate Appraisers and the founder of Ask The Appraiser, a professional community of more than 80,000 real estate agents on Facebook.

Through his appraisal practice and educational platform, John focuses on helping agents and industry professionals better understand the valuation process, bridging the gap between appraisers and the broader real estate community.
column37: Real Estate Agents leave with direct, real-world insight from working appraisers — gaining clarity on how pricing decisions are truly evaluated, how to avoid common deal-killing mistakes, and how to communicate value, comps, and market realities to sellers with greater confidence, credibility, and control.

column2: AIVRE Demo: AI-Assisted Valuation Workflows in Practice
column3:
This demo shows how AIVRE applies AI to support appraisal workflows—helping appraisers organize data, surface insights, and reduce friction—without automating value conclusions or replacing professional judgment.

The goal is to give attendees a realistic understanding of where AI actually helps today, how it integrates with appraisal practice, and what responsibilities remain squarely with the appraiser.
column23: Over Jordan's career, he has worked on several AI-focused projects that eventually gave him the skillset and experience to develop Aivre, the first AI-focused appraisal software. Through Jordan's leadership, Aivre was able to become the first approved vendor by the GSEs and continue on to be one of the most handy UAD 3.6 tools for appraisers.

column2: UAD 3.6 Readiness: What’s Changing and How to Avoid Costly Mistakes
column3:
This session focuses on what UAD 3.6 means in practice — not in theory — and how appraisers can prepare now to avoid workflow disruption and costly errors as implementation continues toward 2027.

While many appraisers have already completed UAD 3.6 reports, real-world use has surfaced friction points, misunderstandings, and common mistakes. This session draws on direct feedback from appraisers to clarify what is actually changing, what issues are showing up most often, and where preparation today can materially reduce risk later.

The emphasis is practical readiness, not compliance for its own sake.

With a skillset including appraisal, banking, regulations, policy, training, licensing, and enforcement, Walitt leads a team of subject matter experts who fill the varying needs of clients.

column2: How the Next Generation of Appraisers Is Actually Innovating
column3:
This session showcases real, working examples of how modern appraisers are innovating inside their practices — not by abandoning standards, but by rethinking how work gets done, how services are positioned, and how time and judgment are protected.

Rather than abstract conversations about “the future,” attendees will hear directly from appraisers who are actively using technology, refined workflows, and strategic positioning to work more efficiently, differentiate their services, and build sustainable careers in today’s market.

The goal is to replace vague innovation talk with practical, replicable ideas.

column22: Appraisal Brothers, Co-Owner / Certified Residential Appraiser
column23:
As Co-Owner and Certified Residential Appraiser at Appraisal Brothers, Joshua Cornejo combines a robust academic background, including a Bachelor's Degree in Political Science and a Master's in Legal Studies, with practical expertise in real estate appraising.

His journey began with mentorship under a USPAP Certified Instructor, followed by gaining valuable insights as a Class I Residential Real Estate Appraiser at the Dallas Central Appraisal District. This experience provided him with a strong foundation in mass appraisal methods, market trends, and property analysis.

Joshua is committed to delivering specialized services—from tax appeal appraisals to measurement services—and ensures Appraisal Brothers utilizes the latest technology for precise valuations and a streamlined client process.

column2: Beyond Chatbots: Agentic AI for Appraisers
column3: This session is designed to help appraisers understand the shift from chatbot-based AI tools to agentic AI workflows that can carry out longer-horizon tasks with greater autonomy. Rather than focusing on AI as a simple prompt-and-response tool, the session will explore how appraisers can begin using AI agents more strategically to support research, analysis, workflow execution, and decision support while maintaining professional judgment and oversight.
column22: AI Transformation Leader in Commercial Real Estate | Keynote Speaker | Helping CRE Firms Shift from Production to Orchestration
column23:
Justin Gohn, CRE, MAI, SRA, founded Gohn & Company in 2006, providing appraisal and consulting services throughout the greater Philadelphia market.

He served as the 2025 President of the Philadelphia Metropolitan Chapter of the Appraisal Institute and currently serves on the Appraisal Institute's National Board of Directors as a 3rd Director.

A keynote speaker and published author in Valuation Magazine, he is a recognized thought leader on artificial intelligence applications in real estate valuation.
column30: CRE, MAI, SRA
column37: Attendees will leave with a clearer understanding of how AI is moving beyond chatbot interactions into agentic workflows, and what that shift means for appraisal practice, productivity, and professional judgment.

column2: The Appraisal Foundation’s View: Standards, Change, and the Road Ahead
column3:
As The Appraisal Foundation approaches its 40th anniversary in 2027, this Main Stage session looks forward — examining where valuation is headed as industry structures evolve and artificial intelligence accelerates change.

From the Foundation’s perspective, standards are not static rules but living frameworks that must remain credible, defensible, and adaptable. This session outlines the Foundation’s long-term vision for the profession, how emerging technology may influence standards and practice, and what appraisers should be preparing for as the next chapter of valuation takes shape.

Led by the President of The Appraisal Foundation, the session provides clarity on the future direction of standards and offers practical guidance for navigating change responsibly while preserving public trust.
column12: Real Estate Appraisers, Aspiring Appraisers, Review Appraisers, AMC Personnel, Collateral Risk Professionals
column23:
Kelly Davids
President, The Appraisal Foundation
Ms. Davids leads The Appraisal Foundation in fulfilling its mission to uphold public trust in the appraisal profession. She was previously the Foundation’s Senior Vice President and has been with the organization for over a decade. Prior to joining the Foundation, Davids was Superintendent of the Ohio Division of Real Estate & Professional Licensing, the chief regulator for Ohio’s appraisers. Her experience includes working in senior-level positions for two Ohio Governors and serving multiple terms in elected office. Davids holds a Master’s degree in Public Policy and Management from The Ohio State University.
column37: Attendees leave with a clear understanding of where the appraisal profession is headed, how standards may evolve alongside emerging technology, and what thoughtful preparation looks like as the industry approaches 2027 and beyond.

column2: Expert Witness Foundations: What Appraisers Need to Know to Get Started
column3: This session gives appraisers a practical introduction to expert witness and litigation work, with a focus on the foundational elements that matter most when getting started. It is designed to help attendees understand how this work differs from traditional lender assignments, how to engage effectively with attorney clients, and how to build the structure, communication, and compensation framework needed before testimony is ever required.
column23: Kyle has been a Certified Residential Appraiser since 2015 and currently serves on the Board of the REAA(Real Estate Appraiser Association).  In that role, Kyle has taught and developed continuing education courses on ADUs, appraiser training, and market analysis.  Kyle became an Accredited Green Appraiser in 2023. Kyle is a proud member of the National Association of Appraisers (NAA) and the Community of Asset Analysts(CAA). He lives with his wife and two daughters in Benicia where he operates a mid-term rental ADU.
column30: AGA, MNAA
column37: Attendees leave with a practical understanding of how to get started in expert witness and litigation appraisal work — including the terminology, attorney communication, compensation structures, and foundational practices that matter most before testimony is ever required.

column2: Why Appraisal Is Still a Great Career — and Why I’d Choose It Again
column3:
This session offers a long-horizon perspective on appraisal as a profession — why it continues to matter, why it remains a viable and meaningful career, and why someone deeply immersed in standards, qualifications, and industry evolution would choose it again.

In a time when the profession is often discussed through the lens of disruption, volume pressure, or technology, this session recenters the conversation on purpose, impact, and professional identity. Drawing on decades of experience at the heart of appraisal standards and policy, Lisa reflects on what appraisal offers that many professions do not.

The goal is to help attendees—especially students, early-career appraisers, and career changers—see appraisal not just as a job, but as a durable professional path.
column22: Vice President of Appraisal Issues, The Appraisal Foundation
column23:
Lisa Desmarais has over 30 years of appraisal experience and is a leader in the industry. Lisa joined The Appraisal Foundation full time in April of 2020 as the Vice President of Appraisal Issues in April 2020. She serves as the technical expert to the Appraisal Standards Board and the Appraiser Qualifications Board. Prior to joining the foundation in this capacity, Lisa served on both The Appraisal Foundation’s Appraisal Standards Board and Appraisal Practices Board.

Prior to 2020, Lisa owned her own residential appraisal firm and was heavily involved in all facets of the appraisal profession. In addition to mentoring aspiring appraisers and teaching seminars, she published several papers and served as a peer reviewer for others in the profession. She was also a member of a number of appraisal task forces and research groups dating back to 2011 and served as an expert witness in numerous hearings and court cases.

In her free time, Lisa enjoys tennis and spending time with her family.
column37: Aspiring Appraisers leave with a renewed understanding of why appraisal remains a strong, respected, and meaningful career—and why professional judgment, independence, and service to the public make it a path worth choosing today and over the long term.
column42: Why Appraisal Is Still a Great Career and Why I'd Choose It Again

column2: Intro to Relocation Appraisal Work
column3:
This session introduces appraisers to the relocation appraisal segment — a specialized area of residential valuation work with distinct client expectations, review standards, and workflow norms.

Relocation work is often unfamiliar to appraisers who have primarily done lender or typical private assignments. This session clarifies how relocation programs function, what the appraiser’s role looks like in practice, and what professionalism and consistency are required to succeed.

The goal is to help attendees understand whether relocation work is a fit for their practice and how to pursue it responsibly and sustainably.
column22: Precision Valuations for Life's Biggest Moves
column23:
Lisa Meinczinger is a highly experienced residential appraiser with over 28 years of expertise in researching, analyzing, and reporting market data across urban, suburban, and rural real estate markets. She is the owner of Advance Appraiser Service, based in Indianapolis, Indiana, where she has provided comprehensive appraisal services since 2011. Her scope of work includes traditional appraisals, appraisal and forensic reviews, hybrid and desktop products, and relocation assignments. She specializes in complex residential properties, litigation support, and high-performance (green/energy-efficient) homes. She is FHA-approved and has supplemental commercial experience under mentorship.
In addition to her appraisal practice, Lisa is an approved instructor for the Appraisal Institute, with nine years of classroom and webinar teaching experience. She regularly teaches courses on a wide range of subjects, including fair housing and valuation bias, market analysis, highest and best use, desktop and hybrid appraisals, rural valuation, and green building valuation. She is approved to teach in all 50 states and remains actively involved in shaping appraisal education content.
Before launching her own business, she served as a staff residential appraiser at Landsafe from 2005 to 2011, where she was instrumental in training new appraisers and uncovering fraud cases in collaboration with Countrywide and the FBI.
She holds multiple prestigious professional designations, including the SRA and AI-RRS from the Appraisal Institute, the ASA from the American Society of Appraisers, and the CRP from Worldwide ERC. She also holds the CDEI designation from the International Distance Education Certification Center and the RAA and GREEN designations from the National Association of Realtors. She maintains her credentials through regular renewals and continuing education.
Lisa earned a Bachelor of Science in Public Affairs from Indiana University Purdue University Indianapolis (IUPUI) in 1996 and completed her initial appraisal education in 1998. She continues to pursue advanced training through the Appraisal Institute and ASA, most recently completing Business Valuation coursework (BV101 in 2022 and BV201 in 2023).
An active leader in the appraisal profession, she serves as the Vice Chair of the Appraisal Institute’s National Board of Directors (2025–2027) and Vice President of the Hoosier State Chapter. She previously served as Trustee for the Appraisal Institute Insurance Trust, Region V Education Liaison, and as a board member in both Indiana and Ohio chapters. She has been deeply involved in the Leadership Development and Advisory Council
(LDAC), including as a discussion leader, and chaired the Candidate Guidance Committee from 2020 to 2024.
Her leadership extends beyond the Appraisal Institute. She is the 2024–2025 President of Relocation Appraisers and Consultants (RAC), having previously served as Vice President and Board Member. She also represents RAC on The Appraisal Foundation Advisory Council (TAFAC), where she is serving as Vice Chair in 2025 after holding the Secretary role in 2024.
As an engaged member of WERC, she has served on the Service Providers Council, Content Committee, Real Estate Work Group, and Conference Planning Committee. She was awarded the Meritorius Service Award in 2025 for volunteer service to the organization. She is also active in the American Society of Appraisers, serving as Secretary / Treasurer for the Real Property Discipline, a member of the Board of Examiners, a conference moderator, and a contributor to education and webinar development.
Dedicated to service both professionally and personally, she has volunteered as a unified dancer with the Special Olympics since 2008.

column2: Pricing Masterclass for Agents: A Repeatable System (Workshop)
column3:
This workshop gives real estate agents a repeatable, evidence-based pricing system they can use consistently across listings—regardless of market conditions.

Rather than relying on intuition or “what feels right,” the session teaches agents how to ground pricing in market evidence, competitive context, and clear communication. 

The focus is practical: how to price well, explain it well, and reduce avoidable price reductions.
column23: Louis J. Pitoni, SRA, RAA, is the owner of Woodland Appraisal Group in State College, Pa. He is an active member and director of the Centre County Association of REALTORS® and the Elk Cameron Board of REALTORS® and currently serves on the National Association of REALTORS®’ Real Property Valuation Committee.

column2: The Evolving Role of State Appraiser Coalitions
column3:
This Main Stage session examines how state appraiser coalitions are evolving — and why their role is becoming more important in a changing regulatory, market, and professional environment.

As appraisal practice faces increased scrutiny, modernization pressures, and shifting state-level priorities, coalitions have emerged as a critical mechanism for coordination, education, and professional representation. 

Drawing on a national vantage point, this session offers insight into what coalitions are seeing on the ground and how appraisers can more effectively engage.
column12: Real Estate Appraisers, Aspiring Appraisers, Review Appraisers, AMC Personnel, Collateral Risk Professionals
column23:
Malinda is the owner/operator of Malinda Griffin & Co. She has been appraising as a Certified Residential Appraiser in the Upstate of South Carolina since 1992 and offering title abstracting services since 1990 with clients that include lending institutions, appraisal management companies, attorneys, accountants, and others. In 2018, Malinda expanded her scope to offer South Carolina Appraiser and Realtor Continuing Education. Malinda served as President of SCPAC in 2020 and continues to serve as Chair of the SCPAC Education Committee. Malinda currently serves on the Board of Directors for NAA (National Association of Appraisers), as President, and is on the following NAA Committees: Appraiser Trainee, Board of Governors, Communications, Conference Program, Executive, Finance, Governmental Affairs, GRH Scholarship, Membership, Appraiser Relief Selection, Nominating and Public Relation Committees’. Malinda is also serving as the 2025 Chair of TAFAC (The Appraisal Foundation Advisory Council). In February of 2025, Malinda was confirmed by the SC State Senate after Governor McMaster’s nomination to the SC Real Estate Appraisers Board. Malinda’s passion for the Appraisal profession shines as she does her part to assist fellow peers stay up to date on the profession/industry through education and industry information. She also has an Appraiser Trainee working under her license. 

Malinda is a member of the National Association of Appraisers (MNAA), the South Carolina Professional Appraisers Coalition (SCPAC), and the Association of Texas Appraisers (ATA). She holds the HMS (Home Measurement Specialist) and Appraisal Review Certification (NAA).

column37: Attendees leave with a grounded understanding of the evolving role of state appraiser coalitions and the NAA — and actionable insight into how professional engagement, coordination, and shared advocacy can help appraisers navigate change and uplift their practice.

column2: Market Analytics for UAD 3.6: How to Support Trends and Segmentation
column3:
This session shows appraisers how to use market analytics intentionally to support trends, segmentation, and conclusions in UAD 3.6–compliant appraisal reports.

As UAD 3.6 shifts emphasis toward clearer structure and data consistency, unsupported or generalized market statements become higher risk. This session focuses on how appraisers can use analytics—not as decoration, but as evidence—to clearly explain market behavior, define segments, and support conclusions in a way reviewers and lenders can follow.

The goal is to help appraisers move from vague narrative to clear, supportable market analysis that travels cleanly through review.
column23: Matthew Hyatt is a State Certified Residential Real Estate Appraiser and Chief Appraiser & General Manager at True Footage, leading national appraisal operations across the U.S. With over two decades of experience specializing in high-end luxury properties, he combines deep valuation expertise with a strong focus on technology, efficiency, and team development. An active industry leader, he serves on multiple boards and committees, including the Appraisal Institute and the National Association of REALTORS®, and is passionate about advancing appraisal standards and education.
column24: Chief Appraiser and General Manager True Footage
column37: Appraisers leave with a practical framework for using market analytics to clearly support trends, segmentation, and conclusions in UAD 3.6 reports—reducing ambiguity and review friction.

column2: True Footage Demo: Measuring Homes Accurately, Consistently, and at Scale
column3:
This demo shows how True Footage is used to capture, verify, and manage property measurements in a way that improves consistency, reduces revision risk, and supports scalable appraisal operations.

The goal is to give attendees a clear view of how accurate measurement data is collected and integrated into appraisal workflows—especially in environments where quality control, documentation, and repeatability matter.
column23: Matthew Hyatt is a State Certified Residential Real Estate Appraiser and Chief Appraiser & General Manager at True Footage, leading national appraisal operations across the U.S. With over two decades of experience specializing in high-end luxury properties, he combines deep valuation expertise with a strong focus on technology, efficiency, and team development. An active industry leader, he serves on multiple boards and committees, including the Appraisal Institute and the National Association of REALTORS®, and is passionate about advancing appraisal standards and education.
column24: Chief Appraiser and General Manager True Footage
column37: Real Estate Appraisers leave with a clear understanding of how standardized, verified measurement data can reduce risk, improve consistency, and support more efficient appraisal workflows.

column2: Housing Right Now: What the Data Says - and What Appraisers Are Seeing on the Ground
column3:
This Main Stage conversation is designed to set the factual and professional foundation for the entire conference.

Before conversations about regulation, technology, AI, or the future of valuation, this session addresses the most pressing question facing appraisers and real estate professionals today:

What is actually happening in housing right now—and what does it mean for the appraisal profession going forward?

By pairing national housing data with on-the-ground market analysis from a practicing appraiser, this session establishes a shared, evidence-based understanding of current market conditions and where housing may be heading next.
column23:
Michael is the Founder and President of PahRoo Chicago Appraisal & Real Estate Consultancy, where for more than two decades he has led one of the Midwest’s premier commercial and residential valuation firms. As Chief Appraiser and a nine-time entrepreneur, Michael specializes in solving complex real estate valuation challenges, bringing firsthand experience in ownership, development, and investment to every assignment Beyond appraisal, Michael is a Bloom Growth Coach and longtime leader within the Entrepreneurs’ Organization (EO), where he trains and mentors business owners on scaling with clarity, structure, and discipline. He also hosts the Amusing Appraisers on Purpose podcast, sharing advanced insights on real estate, finance, and the evolving valuation landscape.

Grounded in rigorous professional designations and shaped by decades of entrepreneurial leadership, Michael brings a rare combination of technical expertise, operational depth, and real-world investment perspective to the future of valuation.
column25: PahRoo Chicago Appraisal & Real Estate Consultancy
column37: Attendees leave with a clear picture of how national housing data and local market conditions are aligning — a practical lens for separating meaningful signals from noise  -  and a stronger basis for applying those insights in valuation, risk management, and strategic decisions ahead.
column42: Housing Right Now: What the Data Says and What Appraisers Are Seeing on the Ground

column2: How Appraisal Review Changes Under UAD 3.6: What Reviewers Actually See
column3: UAD 3.6 is not just a format update, it fundamentally changes how appraisal data is structured, interpreted, and reviewed. This session provides a practical, reviewer-grounded look at how appraisal review evolves in a UAD 3.6 environment, and what that means for appraisal quality, consistency, and defensibility.
column12: Real Estate Appraisers, Review Appraisers, AMC Personnel, Collateral Risk Professionals
column23: Michele is Chief Appraiser at Accurate Group and Secretary of the Industry Advisory Council, with over 20 years of experience across lender, GSE, and capital markets environments. She specializes in appraisal review, risk assessment, and valuation quality, bringing a practical, data-driven perspective on how appraisal analysis is evaluated in today’s evolving regulatory landscape.
column37: Attendees leave with a clearer understanding of how appraisal analysis is evaluated through a Fannie Mae–aligned review lens — and practical insight into what drives quality, consistency, and efficiency in appraisal review and production.

column2: The Responsible Role of AI in Today’s Appraisal Practice
column3:
This session brings together three institutional perspectives shaping the future of residential appraisal practice to examine the responsible role of artificial intelligence today.

As AI tools become more visible across valuation workflows, appraisers face uncertainty around expectations, limits, and professional risk. This panel grounds the conversation by addressing AI through the lenses of practice oversight, standards and ethics, and housing finance policy.

This session recognizes that AI is already being used in various parts of the valuation process and focuses on how appraisers should respond right now as AI becomes part of the valuation ecosystem.
column23:
Michelle is a Certified General Appraiser, AQB Certified USPAP Instructor, and Past Chair of the Appraisal Standards Board at The Appraisal Foundation . A third-generation Realtor-Appraiser, she specializes in residential valuation and has dedicated nearly four decades to advancing professional standards and education within the industry.

Michelle is the founder of Bradley Consultants and presents continuing education courses nationwide, with particular expertise in the Uniform Standards of Professional Appraisal Practice (USPAP), review appraisal, and ethical competency standards. In addition to her instructional work, she performs USPAP-compliant reviews and forensic assignments and has served in leadership roles across multiple appraisal and Realtor organizations

Her career reflects a deep commitment to rigor, ethics, and the long-term credibility of the valuation profession.
column24: Certified General Appraiser and Former Chair, Appraisal Standards Board
column30: SRA, AI-RRS
column37: Attendees leave with a clearer, grounded understanding of how AI fits responsibly into residential appraisal practice — where it can support work, where caution is required, and why professional judgment and accountability remain non-negotiable.

column2: Valuing Luxury Property: A Practical Introduction for Appraisers
column3:
This session introduces appraisers to the realities of valuing luxury residential property — how it differs from typical residential appraisal work and what it takes to do it competently and credibly.

Luxury appraisal is often misunderstood as simply “higher price points.” In practice, it involves greater complexity, thinner markets, less standardized data, and higher expectations from clients, reviewers, and stakeholders. This session explains those differences clearly and shows how appraisers can approach luxury work methodically rather than aspirationally.

The goal is to help appraisers understand whether luxury valuation is a realistic specialization for them and how to begin building the skills and judgment required.
column23:
Michael is the Principal and Chief Appraiser of MJ Valuations, Inc., providing residential real estate appraisal services across the Denver Metro area, including mountain and rural markets, with a focus on highly complex assignments. With nearly four decades in the profession, MJ has held leadership roles at institutions including Bank of America, where he served as District Chief Appraiser, as well as multiple valuation firms before launching and leading his own practice.

He is also a dedicated industry leader, serving as President of the Colorado Coalition of Appraisers and previously as Vice President of both the Coalition and CAREA, helping strengthen professional standards and community across the state. MJ brings deep field experience, technical rigor, and steady leadership to every assignment — combining institutional perspective with boots-on-the-ground expertise in complex residential valuation.
column37: Appraisers leave with a clear, realistic understanding of what luxury residential appraisal actually involves — including how it differs from standard residential work, where this type of work typically arises, and what skills, judgment, and professional habits are required to perform it competently and defensibly.

column2: Integrating Report Writing Support into Your Practice
column3:
This session examines what it actually looks like for an appraiser to integrate report writing and valuation support into their day-to-day practice.

As workload pressure, turnaround expectations, and complexity increase, more appraisers are exploring support models — but many are unclear on how outsourcing works in reality, what tasks can be delegated, and how to maintain quality and control. This session provides a clear, operational view of how appraisal support functions, where it adds value, and where responsibility must remain with the appraiser.

The focus is practical and realistic, grounded in real workflows rather than theory.

column2:
How the Next Generation of Appraisers Is Actually
Innovating
column3:
This session showcases real, working examples of how modern appraisers are innovating inside their practices -  not by abandoning standards, but by rethinking how work gets done, how services are positioned, and how time and judgment are protected. 

Rather than abstract conversations about “the future, ” attendees will hear directly from appraisers who are actively using technology, refined workflows, and strategic positioning to work more efficiently, differentiate their services, and build sustainable careers in today’s market.

The goal is to replace vague innovation talk with practical, replicable ideas.
column22: Machinery Masterclass Institute, Alternative Thinking to Professional Practice
column23:
Patrick Livingston is a seasoned valuation and brokerage professional with over two decades of experience spanning real estate and commercial capital equipment markets across the United States. Since beginning his appraisal career in 2002, he has built a reputation for delivering data-driven, defensible valuations supported by advanced analytical methodologies and deep market insight.

His expertise extends across residential, commercial, rural, and complex asset classes, complemented by extensive experience in capital equipment valuation and machinery brokerage. Patrick has also served in the Fannie Mae capital markets space, where he performed portfolio valuation, loan quality control reviews, and asset disposition analysis on a nationwide scale. His work has contributed to high-level decision-making involving asset performance, risk mitigation, and portfolio optimization.

Throughout his career, Patrick has demonstrated geographic competency across diverse U.S. markets, bringing a consistent, standards-driven approach to valuation assignments regardless of region or asset complexity.

---

Machinery Masterclass Institute

Patrick Livingston is the Founder of Machinery Masterclass Institute (MMI), a global training platform dedicated to advancing excellence in capital equipment valuation and brokerage.

MMI was established to bridge the gap between traditional appraisal education and real-world machinery valuation and brokerage practice. The institute is built on a mission to deliver high-quality, practical, and globally relevant training that empowers professionals to succeed in the capital equipment industry while setting a new standard of excellence through collaboration, education, and professional fellowship.

The leadership team includes:

Cam Cope, President of Machinery Masterclass Institute, an attorney and machinery & equipment appraiser, and partner at VirtuSpect, a leading software company specializing in advanced valuation tools, including Google-based photo recognition technology. His work supports sectors such as securities, railroads, and various government entities, bringing innovation and technological advancement into the valuation space.

Irfan Shaik, Vice President of Machinery Masterclass Institute, a mechanical engineer and government-approved valuer with over 15 years of experience. He is the owner of Associates Pvt. Ltd., with offices across pan-India regions, and has built a strong presence in machinery appraisal and engineering-based valuation throughout India. His expertise spans industrial assets, infrastructure-related equipment, and cross-border valuation practices, contributing significant international depth to the institute’s leadership.

---

Mission & Global Impact

Machinery Masterclass Institute is committed to raising the standard of capital equipment valuation and brokerage training worldwide. Its mission is to provide structured, high-quality education that equips students with both technical valuation skills and practical brokerage strategies.

With a growing global presence, MMI fosters a professional community of students and graduates who are equipped to operate across international markets. The institute emphasizes not only technical excellence but also collaboration, ethical standards, and long-term professional growth.

---

Education & Career Pathways

Students of Machinery Masterclass Institute gain comprehensive training in:

Machinery & equipment valuation methodologies (cost, market, and income approaches)

Asset inspection and documentation techniques

Brokerage strategies and deal structuring

Market analysis and pricing strategies

Report writing and compliance standards

Real-world application through case studies and guided instruction


Graduates are prepared for a range of career opportunities, including:

Independent machinery and equipment appraisers

Capital equipment brokers

Asset managers and consultants

Financial and lending industry valuation specialists

Government and institutional valuation roles


By combining technical rigor with real-world application, Machinery Masterclass Institute positions its graduates to succeed in a rapidly expanding global market for capital equipment valuation and brokerage services.

column3:

Drawing on insider review experience, this session goes beyond checklists and focuses on how appraisal analysis is evaluated in practice. The emphasis is on practical insight that improves both appraisal quality and review effectiveness.

column22: Machinery Masterclass Institute, Alternative Thinking to Professional Practice
column23:
Patrick Livingston is a globally respected authority in capital equipment valuation, appraisal, and machinery brokerage, with more than 24 years of experience as a Certified Appraiser and over 15 years specializing in capital equipment valuation and brokerage. His career bridges institutional appraisal discipline with real-world transactional expertise, positioning him as a trusted leader in the machinery and equipment valuation space.

Patrick has worked extensively with MAI-certified appraisers, contributing to complex and high-level valuation assignments that demand rigorous methodology, analytical precision, and strict adherence to professional standards. His experience also includes professional work with Fannie Mae, where he operated within a highly regulated national framework, supporting valuation-related initiatives and reinforcing best practices for risk management, compliance, and defensible valuation.

Throughout his career, Patrick has serviced national and institutional clients across multiple industries, delivering valuation solutions for asset-based lending, financial reporting, portfolio analysis, litigation support, and strategic asset management. His work is recognized for its credibility, market accuracy, and practical application.

Patrick is the Founder of MMI – Machinery Masterclass Institute, a premier global education and training platform dedicated to advancing excellence in capital equipment valuation, appraisal, and machinery brokerage. MMI was created to address a critical gap in the industry: the need for structured, real-world, market-driven education that goes beyond theory.

About MMI – Machinery Masterclass Institute

MMI – Machinery Masterclass Institute provides world-class training and education for professionals involved in machinery and equipment valuation, appraisal, brokering, lending, and asset management. The Institute delivers practical, experience-based instruction rooted in real market activity and institutional standards.

MMI’s offerings include:

Professional training in machinery and equipment valuation methodologies

Education in Fair Market Value (FMV), Orderly Liquidation Value (OLV), and Forced Liquidation Value (FLV)

Capital equipment appraisal best practices and compliance standards

Machinery brokerage training, including sourcing, pricing, negotiation, and disposition strategies

Market intelligence and applied valuation techniques used by lenders, investors, and institutions

Mentorship and education designed to elevate professionals from foundational knowledge to expert-level execution

Through MMI, Patrick Livingston is building a global standard for capital equipment valuation education, equipping professionals worldwide with the tools, confidence, and credibility needed to perform accurate, defensible, and market-relevant valuations.

Patrick’s mission through MMI is clear: to professionalize, modernize, and elevate the global machinery and equipment valuation industry through education, transparency, and real-world mastery.

This session explains how personal property valuation fits within that landscape — what it is, how it differs from real property appraisal, and where real opportunities exist.

The goal is to help appraisers understand whether personal property valuation is a realistic extension of their practice and how to approach it responsibly.

This session reframes that perception by outlining practical entry points, transferable skills, and early steps that allow appraisers to explore commercial work without abandoning their existing practice.

The goal is to help attendees understand what commercial valuation actually involves and how to approach it methodically, responsibly, and with realistic expectations.

The goal is to help agents think like market analysts: weighing evidence, understanding competition, and making confident, defensible pricing recommendations.

column22: CEO of SFREP, the makers of Appraise-It Pro
column23:
Remington Jones is Chief Executive Officer of Software for Real Estate Professionals, Inc. (SFREP), the makers of Appraise-It Pro. He works closely with SFREP’s founder and owner, R. Wayne Pugh, MAI, CCIM, FRICS, to ensure their software meets and exceeds the needs of residential appraisers across the country.

Now in his eighteenth year with SFREP, Remington was originally hired as a tier-one technical support agent, giving him a frontline view of the challenges appraisers face in their daily work. column2: AI in Valuation: What’s Real, What’s Noise, and What Actually Comes Next
column3:
This Main Stage session cuts through the noise surrounding artificial intelligence in valuation and focuses on how appraisers are actually using AI today — and how that usage is likely to evolve over the next several years.

As AI tools become more accessible and embedded in everyday workflows, the challenge for appraisers is no longer whether AI exists, but how to evaluate its practical value, limitations, and appropriate use. This session provides a grounded, practitioner-focused view of where AI is delivering real utility, where expectations are overstated, and what meaningful change is likely by the end of 2027.

The goal is clarity, not hype — helping appraisers understand what matters now and how to prepare for what comes next.

column2: Effectively Hiring Your First Operations Manager
column3:
This session focuses on one of the most important — and most misunderstood — growth decisions for appraisal firm owners: hiring a first operations manager.

As appraisal businesses grow, many owners find themselves buried in scheduling, coordination, follow-ups, and internal processes that limit their ability to focus on analysis, client relationships, and strategic direction. This session provides clarity on when it actually makes sense to hire an operations manager, what the role should and should not include, and how the right hire can unlock sustainable growth.

The emphasis is practical, realistic, and grounded in real-world firm operations.

The goal is to reduce friction, build trust, and help both sides perform better in high-pressure transactions.

column2: QC & Review in the UAD 3.6 Era: What Will Get Flagged and Why
column3:
This session focuses on how UAD 3.6 appraisals are being reviewed today — and why certain reports are getting flagged in quality control and review workflows.

As structured data plays a larger role in appraisal review, QC systems and reviewers are identifying issues that may not have triggered revisions in the past. This session draws directly from hands-on review experience to explain what is being flagged, why it matters, and how appraisers can address these issues before delivery.

The emphasis is on prevention, clarity, and reducing revision cycles through better upfront reporting.

column2: When Data Is Everywhere: Why Appraisers Matter More in an AI World
column3:
This session reframes the role of the appraiser in an environment where data, models, and automated insights are increasingly abundant.

As AI expands access to information, the perceived value of raw data declines — while the value of interpretation, judgment, and contextual understanding increases. This session explores why appraisers are not being displaced by data-rich systems, but instead are becoming more critical as interpreters, validators, and risk-aware professionals.

The conversation helps attendees understand how professional relevance shifts in an AI-driven landscape — and why human judgment remains essential when data is everywhere.
column23: With 20 years  experience and a passion for residential real estate; the Curd Brothers strive to be leaders in real estate appraisal, investment, and consultancy.
